name: Albert Ammons
abstract: Albert Clifton Ammons (March 1, 1907 – December 2, 1949) was an American pianist and player of boogie-woogie, a blues style popular from the late 1930s to the mid-1940s.

about_quotes.speaker: Ann Rabson
about_quotes.evidence: my dad got me these albums — Pinetop Smith and Albert Ammons, and Meade Lux Lewis and on and on. So that really influenced me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ann Rabson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 21, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: When I was workin' with Albert Ammons and Pete Johnson and Meade Lux Lewis... I was just the vocalist with the group.

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: Dan Qualey... started a label called Solo Art which was the first to record Albert Ammons and Pete Johnson
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Thompson
about_quotes.evidence: Albert Ammons became very influential in my life, and they taught me the boogie-woogie style.

about_quotes.speaker: Al Hall
about_quotes.evidence: I can remember one night Albert Ammons, the (inaudible) Boogie-woogie pianist (inaudible) came in. Barney was standing at the door, and Albert spoke to him. Barney didn't answer him, and he came back to the dressing room crying (inaudible). And we gave him hell.

about_quotes.speaker: Count Basie
about_quotes.evidence: Well, Pete Deke Johnson and Albert Ammons, 'Luxe' Lewis and Luck Liz and (inaudible) -- he played "Honky Tonk Train," what's his name, can't think of the name of it, but I think he would be mentioned in there, because he's made "Honky Tonk Train."
